div.head: The Ambassador in France (Edge) to the Acting Secretary of State
div.p:
403. At 9:30 this evening the meeting took place. Laval, however, was unable to remain except for the first few minutes since he had to
attend the concluding session of the Chamber of Deputies to read the declaration of cloture probably not before 4 o’clock in the morning. We went over the situation with Briand and Flandin. The point concerning the use of the money loaned by France to the German Railway was agreed upon according to the proposals in your telegram No. 322 of July 2, 8 p.m. It was also agreed that the declaration concerning the intention of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York to deposit a credit of 10 million dollars in the Bank for International Settlements in cooperation with the Bank of France and the Bank of England for assisting Central European countries was to be understood with one condition, that those countries which had suffered from the suspension of the conditional payments under the Young Plan should receive priority. This was interpreted to mean that all Central European countries might benefit subject to the proviso concerning priority.

The guarantee fund question and the French interpretation of the Young Plan was briefly discussed. The Minister of Finance reported that his experts were confident of their interpretation and said that the matter was a technical one that would necessarily have to be passed upon by the B. I. S. This whole subject, therefore, could be dropped from consideration for the present.

The only remaining questions were the period for the repayment of the money loaned to Germany during the moratorium year and the question of current contracts for deliveries in kind. The French still insisted on 5 years for the repayment period and no arguments could shake them on this issue. However, they did make the proposal that the payment should be one-fourth each year following the year of suspension, with the added stipulation that if at the beginning of each year the Young Plan was still in force the payment should be only one-eighth. In this way the whole period of payments would be extended to 10 years. Mr. Mellon pointed out that since the United States could not continue the debts owed to it on the contingency of a continued existence of the Young Plan, a proposal of the nature indicated would be entirely unacceptable to the United States. He said further that it would certainly be a great injustice to the debtors of the United States who were in no way concerned with the Young Plan to require them to pay one-fourth of their debts in any given year merely because the Young Plan happened to have been suspended.

Since it seemed impossible to settle the above point, there was next a discussion of the question of the continuation of deliveries in kind under current contracts. We indicated that this seemed to be a matter for regulation by the Germans and the French directly. In a short discussion the French stated their opinion that deliveries in kind were just the sort of assistance to the economic life of Germany desired by President Hoover. Mr. Mellon then suggested that the
two questions, the period of payment and the question of deliveries in kind, might possibly be combined. He asked whether France would not be willing to accept the President’s suggestion of 10 years after the year of suspension and the year of nonpayment if Germany would agree to continue deliveries in kind under current contracts. M. Flandin stated that he felt quite certain that the Council would accept such a suggestion and that he was practically sure of a complete agreement if we could obtain any assurance on this point before the Council meeting tomorrow at 4 o’clock. We promised that we would take up the matter with Washington urgently. The meeting of the Council was set for 4 o’clock tomorrow, and not at an earlier hour because Laval would be unable to leave the Chamber of Deputies much before 6 o’clock in the morning.

We next spent nearly an hour discussing the text of the communiqué, which we will send to you separately.Supra. We tried to obtain a more optimistic tone in the wording of the communiqué, but Laval did not wish to injure at the last moment his position in Parliament or the situation of the French negotiators before the Council tomorrow with an impression that an accord had already been decided upon without them and, so far as that is concerned, without him. The communiqué therefore can be interpreted to the press of the United States as a cold statement of prospects.

The French Government has in mind the calling of a conference of the other countries whose consent is necessary to many of the technical details. M. Briand said that this conference would meet shortly at Paris in order to complete and ratify the technical phases of the agreement which has already been reached in principle between the United States and France.

The Minister of Finance, Mr. Flandin, hopes to receive from us some information on the combining of the questions of deliveries in kind and the period of repayment before tomorrow afternoon when the Council meets. Tomorrow evening after the Council interrogations we will have another meeting with the French negotiators.

In view of Mr. Mellon’s telephone conversation with Mr. Mills and our own feeling we propose to advise Flandin tomorrow during our conference with him that the proposal to have Germany continue deliveries in kind under current contracts involves so many difficulties that it cannot be accepted. We will also tell him that the repayment period of 12 years as set forth by us must apply to all nations and that it is the last proposal that we can make on this subject. Likewise we intend to see Briand prior to the Council meeting and inform him definitely that we can make no further concessions and that if the
Council of Ministers cannot see its way clear to meet the situation which is now definitely presented there seems to be no alternative other than to recommend that the President go ahead with his announced plan of approaching governments individually. We have already intimated this possibility to the conference.
